title: Compare fractions and mixed numbers
description: Practice comparing fractions and mixed numbers that have unlike denominators.
topic_path: Module 5: Fraction equivalence, ordering, and operations
Topic E: Extending fraction equivalence to fractions greater than 1

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$2\dfrac{2}{5}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{34}{10}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{34}{10}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{34}{10}} &= \dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10} + \dfrac{4}{10} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+\dfrac{4}{10} \\  
\\
&=\green{3\dfrac{4}{10}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}}$ and $\green{3\dfrac{4}{10}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

>$\blue{2} <\green{3}$

Because $\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}}$ has a smaller whole number, we do not need to compare the fractional parts.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}} <\green{3\dfrac{4}{10}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{2}{5}} < \green{\dfrac{34}{10}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$4\dfrac{3}{4}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{38}{12}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{38}{12}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{38}{12}} &= \dfrac{12}{12}+\dfrac{12}{12}+\dfrac{12}{12} + \dfrac{2}{12} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+\dfrac{2}{12} \\  
\\
&=\green{3\dfrac{2}{12}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{4\dfrac{3}{4}}$ and $\green{3\dfrac{2}{12}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

>$\blue{4} >\green{3}$

Because  $\blue{4\dfrac{3}{4}}$ has a larger whole number, we do not need to compare the fractional parts.

>$\blue{4\dfrac{3}{4}} > \green{3\dfrac{2}{12}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\blue{4\dfrac{3}{4}} > \green{\dfrac{38}{12}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$3\dfrac{1}{2}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{21}{4}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{21}{4}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{21}{4}} &= \dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4} +\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4} + \dfrac{1}{4} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+1+1+\dfrac{1}{4} \\  
\\
&=\green{5\dfrac{1}{4}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{3\dfrac{1}{2}}$ and $\green{5\dfrac{1}{4}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

>$\blue{3} <\green{5}$

Because $\blue{3\dfrac{1}{2}}$ has a smaller whole number, we do not need to compare the fractional parts.

>$\blue{3\dfrac{1}{2}} <\green{5\dfrac{1}{4}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\blue{3\dfrac{1}{2}} <\green{\dfrac{21}{4}}$

hints:

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{7}{2}} &= \dfrac{2}{2}+\dfrac{2}{2}+\dfrac{2}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+\dfrac{1}{2} \\  
\\
&=\green{3\dfrac{1}{2}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{2\dfrac{3}{12}}$ and $\green{3\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

>$\blue{2} <\green{3}$

Because $\blue{2\dfrac{3}{12}}$ has a smaller whole number, we do not need to compare the fractional parts.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{3}{12}} <\green{3\dfrac{1}{2}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{3}{12}} < \green{\dfrac{7}{2}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$\dfrac{4}{3}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $4\dfrac{1}{6}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{4}{3}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{4}{3}} &= \dfrac{3}{3} + \dfrac{1}{3} \\
\\
&= 1+\dfrac{1}{3} \\  
\\
&=\green{1\dfrac{1}{3}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers  $\green{1\dfrac{1}{3}}$ and $\blue{4\dfrac{1}{6}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

> $\green{1} < \blue{4}$

Because $\green{1\dfrac{1}{3}}$ has a smaller whole number, we do not need to compare the fractional parts.

>$\green{1\dfrac{1}{3}} < \blue{4\dfrac{1}{6}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\green{\dfrac{4}{3}} < \blue{4\dfrac{1}{6}}$

hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{9}{2}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{9}{2}} &= \dfrac{2}{2}+\dfrac{2}{2}+\dfrac{2}{2}+\dfrac{2}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+1+\dfrac{1}{2} \\  
\\
&=\green{4\dfrac{1}{2}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\green{4\dfrac{1}{2}}$ and $\blue{3\dfrac{1}{4}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

>$\green{4} > \blue{3}$

Because $\green{4\dfrac{1}{2}}$ has a larger whole number, we do not need to compare the fractional parts.

>$\green{4\dfrac{1}{2}} > \blue{3\dfrac{1}{4}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\green{\dfrac{9}{2}} > \blue{3\dfrac{1}{4}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$\dfrac{11}{6}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $3\dfrac{1}{3}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{11}{6}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{11}{6}} &= \dfrac{6}{6} + \dfrac{5}{6} \\
\\
&= 1+\dfrac{5}{6} \\  
\\
&=\green{1\dfrac{5}{6}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\green{1\dfrac{5}{6}}$ and $\blue{3\dfrac{1}{3}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

> $\green{1} < \blue{3}$

Because $\green{1\dfrac{5}{6}}$ has a smaller whole number, we do not need to compare the fractional parts.

>$\green{1\dfrac{5}{6}} < \blue{3\dfrac{1}{3}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\green{\dfrac{11}{6}} < \blue{3\dfrac{1}{3}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$1\dfrac{1}{12}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{7}{4}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{7}{4}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{7}{4}} &= \dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{3}{4} \\
\\
&= 1+\dfrac{3}{4} \\  
\\
&=\green{1\dfrac{3}{4}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{1\dfrac{1}{12}}$ and $\green{1\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

$\blue{1} =\green{1}$
Since the whole numbers are the same, we need to compare the fractional parts, $\blue{\dfrac{1}{12}}$ and $\green{\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

Let's change the fractions to have a common denominator of $12$.

$\blue{\dfrac{1}{12}} = \blue{\dfrac{1}{12}}$  

$\dfrac{\green{3}\times3}{\green{4}\times 3} =  \green{\dfrac{9}{12}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we compare their numerators.

$\blue{\dfrac{1}{12}} < \green{\dfrac{9}{12}}$
$\blue{1\dfrac{1}{12}} < \green{1\dfrac{9}{12}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

$\blue{1\dfrac{1}{12}} < \green{\dfrac{7}{4}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$5\dfrac{1}{2}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{23}{4}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{23}{4}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{23}{4}} &= \dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{3}{4} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+1+1+\dfrac{3}{4} \\  
\\
&=\green{5\dfrac{3}{4}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{5\dfrac{1}{2}}$ and $\green{5\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

$\blue{5} =\green{5}$
Since the whole numbers are the same, we need to compare the fractional parts, $\blue{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ and $\green{\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

Let's change the fractions to have a common denominator of $4$: 

$\dfrac{\blue{1}\times 2}{\blue{2}\times 2} = \dfrac{\blue{2}}{\blue{4}}$  

$\dfrac{\green{3}}{\green{4}}= \dfrac{\green{3}}{\green{4}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we compare their numerators.

$\blue{\dfrac{2}{4}} < \green{\dfrac{3}{4}}$
$\blue{5\dfrac{2}{4}} < \green{5\dfrac{3}{4}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

$\blue{5\dfrac{1}{2}} < \green{\dfrac{23}{4}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.** 

$2\dfrac{1}{2}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{28}{10}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{28}{10}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{28}{10}} &= \dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{8}{10} \\
\\
&= 1+1+\dfrac{8}{10} \\  
\\
&=\green{2\dfrac{8}{10}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{2\dfrac{1}{2}}$ and $\green{2\dfrac{8}{10}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

$\blue{2} =\green{2}$
Since the whole numbers are the same, we need to compare the fractional parts, $\blue{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ and $\green{\dfrac{8}{10}}$.

Let's change the fractions to have a common denominator of $10$:

$\dfrac{\blue{1}\times 5}{\blue{2}\times 5} = \dfrac{\blue{5}}{\blue{10}}$

$\dfrac{\green{8}}{\green{10}} = \dfrac{\green{8}}{\green{10}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we compare their numerators.

$\blue{\dfrac{5}{10}} < \green{\dfrac{8}{10}}$
$\blue{2\dfrac{5}{10}} < \green{2\dfrac{8}{10}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

$\blue{2\dfrac{1}{2}} < \green{\dfrac{28}{10}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$4\dfrac{1}{3}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{9}{2}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{9}{2}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{9}{2}} &= \dfrac{2}{2}+\dfrac{2}{2}+\dfrac{2}{2}+\dfrac{2}{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+1+\dfrac{1}{2} \\  
\\
&=\green{4\dfrac{1}{2}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\blue{4\dfrac{1}{3}}$ and $\green{4\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

$\blue{4} =\green{4}$
Since the whole numbers are the same, we need to compare the fractional parts, $\blue{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ and $\green{\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

Let's change the fractions to have a common denominator of $6$:

$\dfrac{\blue{1}\times2}{\blue{3}\times 2} = \blue{\dfrac{2}{6}}$

$\dfrac{\green{1}\times3}{\green{2}\times 3} = \green{\dfrac{3}{6}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we compare their numerators.

$\blue{\dfrac{2}{6}} < \green{\dfrac{3}{6}}$
$\blue{4\dfrac{2}{6}} < \green{4\dfrac{3}{6}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

$\blue{4\dfrac{1}{3}} < \green{\dfrac{9}{2}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$\dfrac{31}{12}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $2\dfrac{1}{2}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{31}{12}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{31}{12}} &= \dfrac{12}{12}+\dfrac{12}{12}+\dfrac{7}{12} \\
\\
&= 1+1+\dfrac{7}{12} \\  
\\
&=\green{2\dfrac{7}{12}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\green{2\dfrac{7}{12}}$ and $\blue{2\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

$\green{2} =\blue{2}$
Since the whole numbers are the same, we need to compare the fractional parts, $\green{\dfrac{7}{12}}$ and $\blue{\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

Let's change the fractions to have a common denominator of $12$: 

$\dfrac{\green{7}}{\green{12}} = \dfrac{\green{7}}{\green{12}} $  

$\dfrac{\blue{1}\times 6}{\blue{2}\times 6} = \blue{\dfrac{6}{12}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we compare their numerators.

$\green{\dfrac{7}{12}} > \blue{\dfrac{6}{12}}$
$\green{2\dfrac{7}{12}} > \blue{2\dfrac{6}{12}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

$\green{\dfrac{31}{12}} > \blue{2\dfrac{1}{2}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$\dfrac{21}{10}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $2\dfrac{4}{5}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{21}{10}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{21}{10}} &= \dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{1}{10} \\
\\
&= 1+1+\dfrac{1}{10} \\  
\\
&=\green{2\dfrac{1}{10}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\green{2\dfrac{1}{10}}$ and $\blue{2\dfrac{4}{5}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

$\green{2} =\blue{2}$
Since the whole numbers are the same, we need to compare the fractional parts, $\green{\dfrac{1}{10}}$ and $\blue{\dfrac{4}{5}}$.

Let's change the fractions to have a common denominator of $10$:

$\dfrac{\green{1}}{\green{10}} = \dfrac{\green{1}}{\green{10}}$  

$\dfrac{\blue{4}\times 2}{\blue{5}\times 2} = \blue{\dfrac{8}{10}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we compare their numerators.

$\green{\dfrac{1}{10}} < \blue{\dfrac{8}{10}}$
$\green{2\dfrac{1}{10}} < \blue{2\dfrac{8}{10}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

$\green{\dfrac{21}{10}} < \blue{2\dfrac{4}{5}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$\dfrac{25}{8}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $3\dfrac{2}{4}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{25}{8}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{25}{8}} &= \dfrac{8}{8}+\dfrac{8}{8}+\dfrac{8}{8}+\dfrac{1}{8} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+\dfrac{1}{8} \\  
\\
&=\green{3\dfrac{1}{8}}
\end{align}$

Now we can compare the mixed numbers $\green{3\dfrac{1}{8}}$ and $\blue{3\dfrac{2}{4}}$.

We start by comparing the whole number part of each.

$\green{3} =\blue{3}$
Since the whole numbers are the same, we need to compare the fractional parts, $\green{\dfrac{1}{8}}$ and $\blue{\dfrac{2}{4}}$.

Let's change the fractions to have a common denominator of $8$:

$\dfrac{\green{1}}{\green{8}} = \dfrac{\green{1}}{\green{8}} $

$\dfrac{\blue{2}\times 2}{\blue{4}\times 2} = \blue{\dfrac{4}{8}}$

Now that our fractions have the same denominator, we compare their numerators.

$\green{\dfrac{1}{8}}  < \blue{\dfrac{4}{8}}$
$\green{3\dfrac{1}{8}} < \blue{3\dfrac{4}{8}}$

This is the correct answer in its original form.

$\green{\dfrac{25}{8}} < \blue{3\dfrac{2}{4}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$3\dfrac{1}{2}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{14}{4}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{14}{4}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{14}{4}} &= \dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4} + \dfrac{2}{4} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+\dfrac{2}{4} \\  
\\
&=\green{3\dfrac{2}{4}}
\end{align}$
The fractional part of $\green{3\dfrac{2}{4}}$ can be simplified.

>$\green{3\dfrac{2}{4}} = \green{3\dfrac{1}{2}}$


Now we can see that the numbers are the same.

>$\blue{3\dfrac{1}{2}} = \green{3\dfrac{1}{2}}$
This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\blue{3\dfrac{1}{2}} {=}\green{\dfrac{14}{4}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Compare the numbers using a $>,<$, or $=$ symbol.**

$2\dfrac{4}{6}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{8}{3}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{8}{3}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{8}{3}} &= \dfrac{3}{3}+\dfrac{3}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3} \\
\\
&= 1+1+\dfrac{2}{3} \\  
\\
&=\green{2\dfrac{2}{3}}
\end{align}$
The fractional part of $\blue{2\dfrac{4}{6}}$ can be simplified.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{4}{6}} = \blue{2\dfrac{2}{3}}$


Now we can see that the numbers are the same.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{2}{3}} = \green{2\dfrac{2}{3}}$
This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\blue{2\dfrac{4}{6}} {=}\green{\dfrac{8}{3}}$

$5\dfrac{4}{5}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $\dfrac{58}{10}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{58}{10}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{58}{10}} &= \dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10}+\dfrac{10}{10}+ \dfrac{8}{10} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+1+1+\dfrac{8}{10} \\  
\\
&=\green{5\dfrac{8}{10}}
\end{align}$
The fractional part of $\green{5\dfrac{8}{10}}$ can be simplified.

>$\green{5\dfrac{8}{10}} = \green{5\dfrac{4}{5}}$


Now we can see that the numbers are the same.

>$\blue{5\dfrac{4}{5}} = \green{5\dfrac{4}{5}}$
This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\blue{5\dfrac{4}{5}} {=}\green{\dfrac{58}{10}}$

$\dfrac{30}{4}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $7\dfrac{1}{2}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{30}{4}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{30}{4}} &= \dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{4}{4} + \dfrac{2}{4} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+1+1+1+1+\dfrac{2}{4} \\  
\\
&=\green{7\dfrac{2}{4}}
\end{align}$
The fractional part of $\green{7\dfrac{2}{4}}$ can be simplified.

>$\green{7\dfrac{2}{4}} = \green{7\dfrac{1}{2}}$


Now we can see that the numbers are the same.

>$\green{7\dfrac{1}{2}} = \blue{7\dfrac{1}{2}}$
This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\green{\dfrac{30}{4}} = \blue{7\dfrac{1}{2}}$

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{34}{6}} &= \dfrac{6}{6}+\dfrac{6}{6}+\dfrac{6}{6}+\dfrac{6}{6}+\dfrac{6}{6}+ \dfrac{4}{6} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+1+1+\dfrac{4}{6} \\  
\\
&=\green{5\dfrac{4}{6}}
\end{align}$
The fractional part of $\green{5\dfrac{4}{6}}$ can be simplified.

>$\green{5\dfrac{4}{6}} = \green{5\dfrac{2}{3}}$


Now we can see that the numbers are the same.

>$\green{5\dfrac{2}{3}} = \blue{5\dfrac{2}{3}}$
This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\green{\dfrac{34}{6}} = \blue{5\dfrac{2}{3}}$

$\dfrac{14}{3}$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $4\dfrac{4}{6}$
hints:
First, we can convert $\dfrac{14}{3}$ to a mixed number.

$\begin{align}\green{\dfrac{14}{3}} &= \dfrac{3}{3}+\dfrac{3}{3}+\dfrac{3}{3}+\dfrac{3}{3}+ \dfrac{2}{3} \\
\\
&= 1+1+1+1+\dfrac{2}{3} \\  
\\

The fractional part of $\blue{4\dfrac{4}{6}}$ can be simplified.

>$\blue{4\dfrac{4}{6}} = \blue{4\dfrac{2}{3}}$


Now we can see that the numbers are the same.

>$\green{4\dfrac{2}{3}} = \blue{4\dfrac{2}{3}}$
This is the correct answer in its original form.

>$\green{\dfrac{14}{3}} = \blue{4\dfrac{4}{6}}$
